text: ligation and interposition of modified martius graft in recto-vaginal fistula repair after low anterior resection for rectal cancer. Here we can see the clinical case, a sixty-three year old woman, operated for rectal cancer by low anterior resection with total mesorectal excision protected with a loop isostomy. Loop ileostomy is closed at six months after a correct anastomotic test. Twelve months later, the patient presented perineal sepsis with passage of gas and stool from vagina.

The clinical examination reveals the internal opening on the anterior face of the anastomosis and a fistulous orifice on the posterior vaginal surface, confirmed by magnetic resonance imaging and anal ultrasound.
The surgeon performs a new loop ileostomy, followed by surgical repair three months later.

Magnetic resonance imaging reveals a rectal tumor infiltrating the rectal walls concentrically, without affecting the mesorectal fat. Sagittal and axial sections show the fistula structure between the rectum and vagina. The patient is positioned in lithotomy under regional anesthesia, with a loose seton visible in the fistula tract.

A transverse incision is made in the rectovaginal space using a Lone Star retractor system for enhanced visualization. Diathermy is used to develop the plane between the external annulus pincer and the posterior vaginal wall, ensuring meticulous hemostasis. The dissection extends to expose the fibrotic fistula structure from the previous anastomosis. A curved probe is maneuvered through the fistula tract to facilitate dissection, which is then encircled, the probe removed, and the tract ligated with absorbable sutures before being divided.

The surgeon uses two taution retractors to achieve a clear dissection of the rectovaginal space, facilitating the muscular interposition. The rectal side of the tract is invaginated with interrupted absorbable sutures, while the vaginal side is similarly ligated. An incision is marked over the labium majus.

It should be noted that Martius described the use of the bulbocavernosus muscle for the treatment of vesicovaginal fistulas, but other authors showed that the bulbocavernosus muscle itself is not necessary to incorporate it in the graft and that the use of the labial adipose tissue may be sufficient to perform the flap. This is called modified Martius graft or labial fat pad.

The surgeon dissects the labial fat flap, ensuring the blood supply from the internal pudendal artery is maintained. The contracting bulbocavernosus muscle is visible. The flap is then tunneled to the rectovaginal septum and positioned over the two-layer fistula closure, secured with multiple sutures to prevent displacement.

A suction drain is placed in the graft space beneath the labial incision, followed by primary closure using Vicryl Quick sutures. The video shows the healed appearance of the surgical site a few months post-operation.
